title: US refusal to renew USMCA trade deal brings uncertainty for Mexico. But the treaty is far from dead
gist: On 1 July 2026 the US declined to renew the USMCA in its current form, opting instead for annual reviews until 2036, but critically the treaty stays in force during renegotiation. Mexico retains a large competitive tariff advantage (about 3.4% weighted-average effective rate) and remains structurally central to US trade, so the outcome is uncertainty rather than existential threat.

insights.claim: On 1 July 2026 the US announced it would not renew the USMCA free trade deal in its current form, even though Mexico and Canada had each confirmed they wished to extend it for a further 16-year term.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Removes the clean 16-year certainty markets and supply-chain planners might have priced, replacing it with a rolling review regime that keeps North American trade risk perpetually on the table.
insights.quote: the US announced it would not renew the US–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA) free trade deal in its current form

insights.claim: The most important outcome is that the USMCA remains in effect during renegotiation, which will now proceed via annual reviews until 2036.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The base case is continuity, not rupture; investors should treat headline 'refusal to renew' language as noise rather than a signal of imminent tariff-wall escalation across North America.
insights.quote: The fact that the treaty will remain in place during the renegotiation is hugely significant in its own right.
insights.locator: The US refusing to renew the USMCA

insights.claim: The treaty's duration is resolved in principle to stay in force at least until 2036, and if no agreement is reached by then it would expire, though a lapse without a replacement is considered unlikely given North American economic integration.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The 2036 backstop gives a decade-long runway of tariff-preferential access, but the annual-review cadence means each year carries renegotiation risk to monitor.
insights.quote: If the parties have not reached an agreement by 2036, the treaty will expire

insights.claim: The current renegotiation, unlike the NAFTA-to-USMCA transition, has revolved around revising rather than abolishing the agreement, which is far less existentially damaging even if worse than the treaty's ideal 'simple' revision.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames the political ceiling of downside risk: the negotiating floor is a modified deal, not a collapse to WTO tariffs, capping worst-case exposure for Mexico-linked assets.
insights.locator: So far, Mexico has been relatively successful

insights.claim: Around 88 per cent of Mexican exports to the US enter duty-free under the USMCA, leaving the weighted-average effective tariff on Mexican exports at only about 3.4 per cent despite headline rates as high as 25 per cent on steel and aluminium.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The gap between scary headline tariffs and the low blended effective rate is the real signal for costing Mexican-sourced goods; sector-specific steel/aluminium exposure is the exception, not the rule.
insights.quote: the weighted-average effective tariff on Mexican exports is only about 3.4 per cent
insights.locator: Although US tariffs on Mexico are higher

insights.claim: Mexico's roughly 3.4 per cent effective US tariff is far below comparable economies, with China at about 22 per cent, India at 8.4 per cent and Brazil at 10.6 per cent.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Quantifies Mexico's nearshoring moat: a multi-hundred-basis-point tariff edge over China and other EM manufacturers reinforces the case for continued supply-chain relocation into Mexico.
insights.quote: the average effective tariff on Chinese exports to the US now stands at about 22 per cent, while comparable economies such as India and Brazil face 8.4 per cent and 10.6 per cent respectively

insights.claim: Mexico is the main trading partner of the US and ranks first-to-third for 36 of the 50 states, while the US absorbs roughly 80 per cent of Mexican exports.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The deep bilateral interdependence and domestic-political spread across 36 states creates structural resistance to punitive US action, underpinning the durability thesis for Mexico trade exposure.
insights.quote: ranks between the first and third largest trading partner for 36 out of the 50 states
insights.locator: Mexico is also the main trading partner

insights.claim: On 2 April 2025 'Liberation Day', Trump imposed 25 per cent tariffs on non-USMCA-compliant goods and 10 per cent on non-compliant energy against Mexico, explicitly tied to stopping illegal migration and drug flows rather than trade issues.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows tariffs are being used as a security lever, so trade-exposed positions must be watched against migration and fentanyl metrics, not just economic data.
insights.quote: Trump announced tariffs of 25 per cent on non-USMCA-compliant goods and 10 per cent on non-compliant energy against Mexico for its failure to stop flows of illegal migration and drugs across the border

insights.claim: Migration encounters fell from more than 2 million in 2022 to 237,538 in 2025 and US fentanyl deaths halved over the same period, giving the US grounds to suspend new tariffs on Mexico.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: These are the leading indicators that unlocked tariff relief; sustained improvement supports the low-effective-tariff regime, while any reversal would be the trigger for renewed tariff escalation.
insights.quote: migration flows have been significantly reduced from more than 2 million encounters reported by US authorities in 2022 to 237,538 in 2025, while US fentanyl deaths were halved during the same period

insights.claim: Negotiations have been predominantly bilateral even though Mexico and Canada are both pushing for more trilateral talks.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Bilateral structure lets Washington divide and pressure partners individually; a shift toward trilateral talks would signal Mexico and Canada gaining coordinated leverage.
insights.quote: Negotiations between the US and its two treaty partners have thus far been predominantly bilateral
insights.locator: Negotiations between the US and its two treaty partners

insights.claim: Washington is expected to press for measures to narrow its trade deficit, raise US content in regional supply chains and tighten rules of origin to curb trans-shipment, while Mexico pushes to broaden tariff exemptions and confine tariffs to as few sectors as possible.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Tighter rules of origin and higher regional-content thresholds are the concrete channel through which Chinese components in Mexico-assembled goods get squeezed, a key risk for trans-shipment-reliant supply chains.
insights.quote: raise US content in regional supply chains and tighten the rules of origin to curb trans-shipment
insights.locator: Mexico is likely to continue bilateral negotiations

insights.claim: Tariffs will persist regardless of the negotiation outcome and markets already know this.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets expectations that a zero-tariff return is off the table, so the tradeable variable is the breadth and level of tariffs by sector, not their existence.
insights.quote: it is important to remember that there will still be tariffs – and markets know this
insights.locator: What kind of treaty it is will depend
